title: IRS Code 640 Meaning (Credit Applied / Credit Transfer Reversal) – For Tax Professionals
description: IRS Code 640 signals a credit applied or a credit transfer reversal. See how it ties to codes 766, 768, and 806, and document it in a client-ready report.
content:
IRS Transcript Codes • Built for 750,000+ U.S. Tax Professionals

IRS Code 640 Meaning

IRS Code 640 generally signals credit movement on the tax module (commonly a credit applied or a credit transfer reversal). This page explains when it shows up, what it usually means in context, and how to summarize it cleanly for a client deliverable. Want to see what clients get? Open the sample report, then run your own transcript when you’re ready.

Try the parser (requires credits)

What is IRS Code 640?

Plain-English definition: IRS Code 640 usually reflects a credit being applied, moved, or reversed on the tax module. The practical meaning comes from the amount, posting date, and what credit source codes sit around it.

Common transcript type
Account Transcript
Usually appears near
Codes 766, 768, 806
Typical context
Credit application, credit corrections, module balancing
Client-friendly summary
"A credit was applied or adjusted on your account"

When does Code 640 appear?

  • After credits post: It can show after common credits (withholding, refundable credits, adjustments) hit the module and the IRS applies or re-applies them.
  • During corrections: It can appear when the IRS reverses or corrects how a credit was previously applied (often tied to timing, offsets, or reallocations).
  • When balancing modules: It may appear as part of internal account balancing when a credit moves between periods or modules.

What does Code 640 usually signal?

If this appears alone

Treat it as a credit movement marker. Without surrounding context (credit source, balance change, or later outcomes), it does not confirm a refund or a final resolution.

If this appears with Code 806 or 766/768

This often indicates the IRS is applying, reallocating, or correcting credits sourced from withholding (806) or refundable/other credits (766/768). Compare amounts and dates to see whether the credit reduced a balance due, increased a credit position, or reversed a prior application.

If this appears with Code 290 or 300

A credit movement alongside an assessment often means the IRS is netting credits against a newly assessed amount. The key is whether the net result is a balance due, a refund position, or a neutral balance.

If this appears with Code 570

Credit movement can occur even when a hold exists. If 570 is present, set expectations that the refund outcome may be delayed until the hold releases, even if credits look favorable.

What should a tax professional do next?

  1. Confirm the math: Review the module balance before/after 640, then reconcile amounts against nearby credit source codes.
  2. Identify the credit source: Tie the movement to 806 (withholding) or 766/768 (credits) where possible, using dates and equal-and-opposite amounts.
  3. Document clearly: Summarize what moved, why it likely moved (application vs correction), and what outcome to watch for (refund, offset, balance due, hold release).

Example: how this looks in a parsed report

Below is a simplified view of how Code 640 can appear in a client-ready transcript report with context:

Transcript Credit Activity (Simplified)
Code 806 – Withholding credit
Amount: $3,200
Code 640 – Credit applied / credit transfer reversal
Date: 05/10/2024
Code 766 – Credit to your account
Note: Used to reconcile credit sourcing

FAQ

What does IRS Code 640 mean on a tax transcript?

IRS Code 640 generally reflects credit activity on the module, commonly showing a credit applied to the account or a credit transfer reversal. The meaning depends on the amount, posting date, and surrounding transaction codes.

Does IRS Code 640 mean my client is getting a refund?

Not necessarily. Code 640 shows credit movement, not a refund release. Confirm whether the credit is being applied to a liability, transferred, or later followed by refund outcome codes. Always review the module balance and adjacent credits.

How does Code 640 relate to credits like 806, 766, and 768?

Codes 806, 766, and 768 represent common credit sources. Code 640 may appear when a credit is applied, moved, or reversed in relation to those credits. Compare amounts and dates to determine whether the credit reduced balance due, shifted between periods, or corrected a prior application.

What should I check when Code 640 appears?

Confirm the posting date, amount, current balance, and whether the credit is tied to a specific source code (806/766/768). Check for later adjustments and document the credit movement clearly for the client.
